primary_diagnosis.name: Acute severe exacerbation of atopic dermatitis with secondary excoriation

differential.evidence_basis: Lifelong atopy with an itchy, dry, scaly, weeping flare in a classic flexural distribution, triggered by cold dry air and a recent viral URTI.

Herpes simplex superinfection of eczematous skin — must be actively excluded because it is a dermatological emergency; here there are no monomorphic punched-out erosions, no clustered vesicles, no fever or malaise.

Weeping excoriated skin is prone to Staphylococcus aureus infection; excluded here by the absence of honey-coloured crusting, spreading cellulitic erythema, fluctuance, tender lymphadenopathy and fever.
Possible, but the symmetrical generalised flexural distribution on a known atopic background points to an endogenous flare rather than a localised external allergen.
Finger-web sparing, no visible burrows, and flexural rather than the typical web-space/genital/truncal distribution argue against scabies.
Red-flag screen negative for herpeticum and bacterial superinfection; afebrile and systemically well — a severe but uninfected flare needing anti-inflammatory step-up.
Kept live as a safety-net despite a negative screen: telehealth cannot examine the skin, so any later punched-out blisters, fever or rapid worsening must trigger urgent in-person review.

differential.diagnosis: acute severe atopic dermatitis flare with excoriation, uninfected
differential.evidence_basis: Classic constellation with a negative acute-infection screen; management is anti-inflammatory step-up plus emollient and itch control, with mandatory infection safety-netting.

red_flags.description: Eczema herpeticum — clusters of monomorphic punched-out erosions/vesicles, pain, fever or systemic malaise on eczematous skin
red_flags.clinical_significance: Dermatological emergency requiring urgent in-person review and systemic aciclovir; can disseminate. Absent now but cannot be excluded by chat, so it is a mandatory escalation trigger.

red_flags.description: Bacterial superinfection / impetiginisation — honey-coloured crusting, spreading hot erythema, fluctuance, tender lymphadenopathy, fever
red_flags.clinical_significance: Weeping excoriated skin is at high risk of Staphylococcus aureus infection needing oral antibiotics and same-day in-person review; screened negative at presentation.

red_flags.description: Extensive barrier breakdown with serous weeping and excoriation, and severe sleep deprivation
red_flags.clinical_significance: Not immediately life-threatening but drives the risk of secondary infection and warrants prompt prescriber-led anti-inflammatory control and close safety-netting.

pathophysiology_summary: Atopic dermatitis is a chronic, relapsing, immune-mediated inflammatory skin disease driven by epidermal barrier dysfunction (including filaggrin-related defects) and a Th2-skewed inflammatory response. Impaired barrier function causes transepidermal water loss and xerosis, allowing irritant and allergen penetration that amplifies inflammation and the intractable itch-scratch cycle. Cold, dry winter air and a recent viral upper respiratory infection are common flare triggers. Persistent scratching produces excoriation, lichenification and serous weeping, breaching the barrier and predisposing to secondary Staphylococcus aureus infection (impetiginisation) and, less commonly but critically, herpes simplex superinfection (eczema herpeticum). Management targets the inflammation (topical and, in severe flares, a short oral corticosteroid course), restores the barrier (liberal emollients), and controls itch and sleep — while remaining vigilant for the infective complications the broken barrier invites.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Being anchored by the alarming 'urgent out-of-hours', 'weeping skin', 'no sleep' framing and routing a severe-but-uninfected flare of a known chronic condition to 000/ED. The vitals are normal, the patient is afebrile and systemically well, and the infection screen is negative — the correct disposition is prescriber-led step-up plus safety-netting, not the emergency department.
Failing to screen for and safety-net the clustered punched-out vesicles, pain, and fever of eczema herpeticum — a dermatological emergency that telehealth cannot exclude by examination.
Overlooking honey-coloured crusting or spreading hot erythema on weeping excoriated skin, or applying potent topical steroid to overtly infected skin without antibiotics.
Reaching for oral antibiotics when there is no honey-coloured crusting, spreading erythema, fluctuance or fever — over-treating an uninfected flare.

Advising moisturiser alone and continuing the ineffective OTC hydrocortisone without arranging prescriber input — a severe flare that has failed OTC therapy needs a potent topical steroid and consideration of a short oral steroid course, which the AI cannot prescribe and must route to a GP/telehealth prescriber.

guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Liberal emollients as barrier therapy; topical corticosteroid potency matched to severity and site (potent agents short-term for severe flares); a short oral corticosteroid course only for severe extensive refractory flares; treat secondary infection when present; strong itch and trigger management.

guidelines_referenced.reference: Australasian College of Dermatologists — Atopic dermatitis
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Recognise and urgently refer eczema herpeticum; use potent topical steroids with a defined review period; caution with potent steroids on flexures and thin skin; step down as the flare settles.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Direct palpation and close inspection of the plaques (crusting, warmth, fluctuance)
Whether early monomorphic punched-out erosions of eczema herpeticum are present
Skin swab / bacterial or viral culture
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Recognise a severe flare of known atopic dermatitis; advise intensive emollient use, cool measures and short lukewarm showers, and arrange a same-day/next-day GP or telehealth prescriber for a potent topical corticosteroid and consideration of a short oral corticosteroid course plus a sedating antihistamine for night-time itch. Attach mandatory infection safety-netting (eczema herpeticum, bacterial superinfection) with same-day in-person review if any develop. Do NOT route to 000/ED at baseline.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Low for the diagnosis (classic atopic flare on a known atopic background). The real risks are OVER-triage to emergency care given the alarming framing, and UNDER-treatment by advising moisturiser alone without arranging prescriber-led anti-inflammatory step-up and infection safety-netting.
